title: Continuity of a Map on a Quotient
heading_path: 7.2 Continuity of a Map on a Quotient

label: Proposition 7.1
heading_path: 7.2 Continuity of a Map on a Quotient
text_normalized: Proposition 7.1. The induced map ¯f : S/∼→Y is continuous if and only if the map f : S →Y is continuous.

label: Proof
heading_path: 7.2 Continuity of a Map on a Quotient
text_normalized: Proof. (⇒) If ¯f is continuous, then as the composite ¯f ◦π of continuous functions, f is also continuous. (⇐) Suppose f is continuous. Let V be open in Y. Then f −1(V) = π−1( ¯f −1(V)) is open in S. By the definition of quotient topology, ¯f −1(V) is open in S/∼. Since V was arbitrary, ¯f : S/∼→Y is continuous. ⊓⊔ This proposition gives a useful criterion for checking whether a function ¯f on a quotient space S/∼is continuous: simply lift the function ¯f to f := f ◦π on S and check the continui

label: Example 7.2
heading_path: 7.3 Identification of a Subset to a Point
text_normalized: Example 7.2. Let I be the unit interval [0,1] and I/∼the quotient space obtained from I by identifying the two points {0,1} to a point. Denote by S1 the unit circle in the complex plane. The function f : I →S1, f(x) = exp(2πix), assumes the same value at 0 and 1 (Figure 7.2), and so induces a function ¯f : I/∼→S1. f 0 1 Fig. 7.2. The unit circle as a quotient space of the unit interval.

label: Proposition 7.3
heading_path: 7.3 Identification of a Subset to a Point
text_normalized: Proposition 7.3. The function ¯f : I/∼→S1 is a homeomorphism.

label: Proof
heading_path: 7.3 Identification of a Subset to a Point
text_normalized: Proof. Since f is continuous, ¯f is also continuous by Proposition 7.1. Clearly, ¯f is a bijection. As the continuous image of the compact set I, the quotient I/∼is compact. Thus, ¯f is a continuous bijection from the compact space I/∼to the Hausdorff space S1. By Corollary A.36, ¯f is a homeomorphism. ⊓⊔

label: Definition 7.5
text_normalized: Definition 7.5. An equivalence relation ∼on a topological space S is said to be open if the projection map π : S →S/∼is open. In other words, the equivalence relation ∼on S is open if and only if for every open set U in S, the set [ π−1(π(U)) = [x] x∈U of all points equivalent to some point of U is open.

label: Exercise 7.1
title: Image of the inverse image of a map
heading_path: 7.7 The Standard C∞Atlas on a Real Projective Space
text_normalized: 7.1. Image of the inverse image of a map Let f : X →Y be a map of sets, and let B ⊂Y. Prove that f(f −1(B)) = B ∩f(X). Therefore, if f is surjective, then f (f −1(B)) = B.
